title: Full Fine-Tuning vs. LoRA vs. QLoRA
subtitle: Three ways to adapt a pretrained model — same weight matrix, three different update strategies.
source_attribution: Derived from this chapter's own reviewed prose; see the chapter's source_attribution for the underlying upstream reference. See CREDITS.md.

methodCards.name: Full fine-tuning
methodCards.tagline: Update every weight in the model directly
bullets: All weights trainable, no frozen matrix
Highest compute, memory, and storage cost

methodCards.name: LoRA
methodCards.tagline: Freeze W, learn a low-rank additive update
bullets: Only A and B are trainable — 2×d×r params instead of d²
One frozen base serves many hot-swappable adapters

methodCards.name: QLoRA
methodCards.formula: W stored as NF4 (4-bit); A, B still full precision
bullets: Frozen base needs no optimizer state at all
Fits a 7B fine-tune on a single 24 GB consumer GPU
Small quantization error; slower per-step (on-the-fly dequant)

fullft.value: Large domain shift, lots of compute

whenToChoose.heading: Choose full fine-tuning when
bullets: The task needs a behavior shift too large for any adapter
You need the highest possible ceiling on quality

whenToChoose.heading: Choose LoRA when
bullets: The base model already performs reasonably well
You need many task-specific adapters on one shared base
You want fast, cheap experimentation and easy rollback

whenToChoose.heading: Choose QLoRA when
bullets: The model doesn't fit in memory at bf16
You're limited to a single consumer-class GPU
A small quantization-error tradeoff is acceptable

workedExample.heading: Parameter count for a 7B model (query + value, 32 layers, rank 16)
workedExample.body: Each targeted 4,096×4,096 matrix is 16,777,216 params; across 64 matrices (q and v, 32 layers) that's ≈1.074B params for full fine-tuning. A rank-16 LoRA adapter replaces each matrix with A (16×4,096) and B (4,096×16) — 131,072 trainable params per matrix, 8.39M total across all 64 matrices. That's about 0.78% of what full fine-tuning would touch.

coreIntuition.heading: Why a low-rank update is enough
coreIntuition.body: Most useful weight updates during fine-tuning live in a much lower-dimensional subspace than the full weight matrix. A thin pair of matrices — A and B — can capture that subspace directly, so a fraction of a percent of the original parameter count can still shift behavior meaningfully. Why change every number in W when a few well-placed low-rank directions achieve the same effect?

interviewQuestions.question: Walk me through why a single LoRA adapter costs 2×d×r parameters rather than d².
interviewQuestions.hint: And what happens to the total as you target more matrices across more layers?

interviewQuestions.question: When would you reach for full-parameter fine-tuning instead of LoRA, even knowing it costs more?
How does QLoRA keep training stable while the base model sits in 4-bit precision?
interviewQuestions.hint: NF4, double quantization, paged optimizer states.

productionConsiderations.text: Multi-adapter serving: batch requests bound for different adapters and apply each request's A/B matrices on the fly on one resident base model.
Alpha and rank move together — changing rank without adjusting alpha silently changes the effective update magnitude.
QLoRA's on-the-fly dequantization adds real per-step latency that bf16 LoRA doesn't pay.
Target-module choice (all linear layers vs. just query/value) trades stability and quality against adapter size.

checklist.text: Rank chosen to match task complexity, not picked arbitrarily
Alpha scaled with rank (or RS-LoRA used at high rank)
Target modules chosen deliberately, not defaulted
VRAM budget checked against the actual GPU available
Multi-adapter serving strategy planned before scaling adapter count

keyTakeaways: LoRA freezes W and trains a 2×d×r-parameter low-rank update instead of d²
QLoRA compresses only the frozen base to 4-bit — adapters still train at full precision
Rank and alpha move together; retune alpha whenever you change rank
Multi-adapter serving on one shared base is LoRA's real production advantage, not just cheaper training

nodes.label: Does the base model fit your GPU at bf16?
Does the task need a large behavior shift?
